speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 7th of February, 1996. The survivor is Leonie Hilton. The interviewer is Jacki Wade. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language of the interview will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Wednesday, February the 7th, 1996. My name is Jackie Wade. I'm interviewing Leonie Hilton in Sydney, Australia. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello, your name is Leonie Hilton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's spelled L-E-O-N-I-E.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: H-I-L-T-O-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born April 13, 1916.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name was Hilschberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilschberg, H-I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spelled H-I-L-S-C-H-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now, Leonie, I'm wondering if you can tell me about your childhood, what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember not terribly much. I had a stepmother, who did not-- who was not very fond of me. But I had a little sister four years younger, and she was very pretty. I was an ugly duckling. And my sister was very pretty, with curly hair, long hair, and also rather clever. She's still alive but lives in Germany. She has never sought-- it's uninteresting, anyway. And I was very keen on sport. And I was a very good sports girl. My learning was comme ci, comme ca. [LAUGHS] But I started tennis when I was 10 years old. As a matter of fact, I met my husband John on the tennis courts here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little, if you can-- do you remember the house that you grew up in at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a beautiful, big, very big unit. And my late father was a solicitor and a barrister. And he was very busy. We had three rooms from our 10-- 10-room mansion that was the office. That was more or less at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a huge apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge apartment -- 10 rooms. And there's very little I can say, because when I came home from school, I took my bicycle, and off I went, you know, or went to the tennis court, picked up balls, whatever, you know. I was not very happy at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think-- I presume that you had-- in terms--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea that I'm Jewish. That was never a question. My late father was-- he got such a-- with a sword, they made-- they were fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were fighting, and he had a beautiful cut here. And of that, he was very proud. He was a rather good-looking man. I loved him. But he was very busy with his law. And my stepmother was nondescript.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father lived as a Jew, or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Oh my God, not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he baptized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were baptized, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your stepmother was not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you-- do you remember your own mother at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I used to see her on school holidays. She used to live in another town. And she remarried. And I saw her, but I was not very close to her, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a difficult situation for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. Yes and no. You know, as stepmother, she had her own child. And I was rather an ugly duckling as a child. And she always tried to put me in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you think of the-- looking back, what do you think the marriage between your father and your stepmother was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had absolutely no Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Christmas we went to church. It was a very nice church on the lake. There is a lake where we lived. We looked onto the lake. And it was-- the church was gorgeous, very old-fashioned, church of England sort of religion, very easy religion, not complicated. And every Christmas, we went to church. And my father did, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you would have had absolutely no contact with Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what was Jewish people. I had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never worried about it. It never--

speaker: interviewer
text: It never occurred to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never occurred to me. And when we were smaller, we had different girls to look after, the two girls we were. And she took us to her Catholic Church. I'd like it very much. [LAUGHS] You know, I had a very-- it was never discussed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the family-- as a family, going on holidays at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we went with stepmother. Father never came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: East-- no, what is it? Austria-- Baltic Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what did you do there, what sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we swam and what you do in surfer's paradise. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK, and so life went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went from school days to getting work or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then it came-- was already in 1933, don't forget, Hitler came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That affected me that we had to bring-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], what you call it? --a document that we are not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first time you'd heard that word?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I said to Father or Stepmother, listen, we have to bring a document to school. And Father-- he said, I give it to you tomorrow or the day after tomorrow. And he put it off for a bit. He didn't tell me. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked again, because they were asking what is happening. And he said, I can't give you. That's the first time I heard. At the end of the school year, to go up to the next class, which would have been [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I don't know what that is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it sounds like first-- high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, no, not first-year high school. I was already--

speaker: interviewer
text: More, more.

speaker: interviewee
text: --more, more, higher, before the matriculation.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so about-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already--

speaker: interviewer
text: 16, maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 15, 16, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, I can't give you the letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that start to make you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so upset. I can't tell you. And went over to my girlfriend, who we sat together at the school years. And the mother opened the door and she said, I heard you are a bloody pig, a Jewish pig. Don't come here again. And she closed the door in my face. I stood there like an ox. I didn't understand what happened. My best girlfriend's mother, she made-- she looked after me as I was her child. From one day to the other, she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: She turned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Turned about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a very upsetting incident.

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, terrible, terrible upsetting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father understand this? And did he try and spend time with you and explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He just said, don't talk about it. I said, listen, I should not talk about it? The whole class beat me up, more or less. I couldn't go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had no friends? You didn't stay at school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your education finished there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My education-- I went to a school-- a school for-- to learn typing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And through my father, through my old papa, he had a lot of friends, and he always said, don't mention that you are Jewish. Don't mention that you are Jewish. It was like poison. And so that's-- so I went to school to learn commercial art, more or less. Typing and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shorthand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shorthand, yes. I learned all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you something. Did you ever see your friend again? Your best friend-- did you ever see her again, or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one friend. That's another one. That with the mother, I never saw again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never saw her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lost a lot of friends through that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely-- nearly all.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very devastating--

speaker: interviewee
text: Except one. And one was in love with a Jewish boy. He was-- he was working in a nightclub playing the piano, beautiful. And this girl had a very strong Nazi father. And she loved that Jewish boy. And she said she's going to follow him wherever we go. So we had to decide what to do. She was a very, very rich girl. And she said, look, I might-- I might for show get engaged to this very rich man my father picked for me. And we were in the school together. But she was one year younger than I. Anyway, and she saw him in the nightclub. And that was the one she wanted. So he was going to Shanghai. Now we had to get money for my trip. I said, I'm not going to stay. I had to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know how old you were, if you can remember, about this time. Are you 18, 20?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was already 19 or 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're planning to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go. I had already a few jobs. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father or your stepmother try to stop you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't know very much what I was doing. I was still living at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't talk much with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Because we were not-- even though she came here with my late father-- just for a few days they were here. And she tried to make how-- what a lovely girl I was, and how she looked after me. But it's all rubbish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your memories are different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, quite different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me something. Your stepsister, though, how did she feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my real sister. That wasn't my stepsister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your real sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my real sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: She--

speaker: interviewer
text: About you, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: About me? All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would see her. I've seen her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband knows her. And she, unfortunately, was hidden during the occupation of Berlin. And she is a bit--

speaker: interviewer
text: --disturbed from that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she's very much disturbed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have interrupted you. I'm sorry. So you were trying to get together the money to go to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we had to make a plan. I don't think my husband ever heard that. I had to-- to be-- I had to fall into a trance or something on the floor. Because in his office, in that boyfriend's office, the Ayran one she was supposed to marry-- her father wanted that. He was a very wealthy man. And she said to her fiance then, listen, we have to do something. This girl has to leave. You know what's going on here in Germany. And she found out she is Jewish. We have to help her to get out. And this-- so I had to train to become--

speaker: interviewer
text: Faint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Faint. So I fainted beautiful in his office. And so Sonia said, you see, we have to help. And you must help me. Otherwise, I won't marry you. And he was very keen on that girl. Had no idea that she is-- has somebody else. Anyway, we did that beautifully. I fell to the floor. They revived me with water and what have you. And he said, don't worry, don't worry. I give you the money, what you need for your trip, and everything will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, my girlfriend-- her name was Bronia-- said, at least one we have. Now we have got money for one ticket. I find my way for the other ticket as well. And so I left by train Berlin. They all came to the-- to see me off. Your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family didn't see me off. They didn't even know I was going. [LAUGHS] I was-- I never had a nice home life, even as a child-- not at all. But stepmother was not delighted with me, you know. I wasn't her type. [LAUGHS] So that's all I can say. Anyway, and my girlfriend-- and you want to know about my trip?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know everything, all about the trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my girlfriend didn't have, but I had some money for my ticket. I packed one suitcase. And I left on my own after the Kristallnacht. When I saw the Kristallnacht, what happened there and how the streets looked, you wouldn't believe it, that that is possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- Bronia-- that was this girlfriend, told me, I don't want you to stay another minute. As soon as you can get out, you have to go out. So I had my ticket, I had 10 pounds, and that was my fortune. With that, I left. And did you have papers stating that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they put the big J into my passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're traveling as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And England wouldn't let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt a bit funny. But I had-- I knew I had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: No choice.

speaker: interviewee
text: No choice. No choice. My best-- other girlfriends wouldn't look at me anymore. I mean, it was-- they were all of a sudden very anti-Semitic, except that one. And this one came to see me in Singapore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I went on the boat in Naples. I had to go by train because England would not let me in to fetch the boat from Southampton, I think-- Southampton. The boat left from Southampton to go to Shanghai but via Italy. And I've forgotten the name of the town in Italy where I was going to fetch the boat. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went on the train, and I come to the German frontier. Biel in Switzerland has two-- two-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm losing words-- two apartments for going, German side and Swiss side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the German side, all of a sudden the door opened, and a big Nazi officer comes in. You here I hear is a Jewish pig! German Nazi officer. A Jewish pig. And he said, who is that Jewish pig? And I said, I am. And the whole compartment started to look at me and didn't want to believe it. Open your case! I opened my case. Ah, he said, that's a nice little outfit. I take to my bride. Took that out, threw the passport on the floor, and said, no, we went the Swiss side. And the people in the train were flabbergasted. They put a towel around me. I started to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I couldn't believe it, you know. He took some things from my suitcase. But the rest I got back. And now we were in-- this was Naples where I caught the boat. It just came to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the people were shocked on the train at that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Switzerland. They were absolutely shocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone say anything to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To him? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Too frightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Too frightened. Very big man, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And probably with a gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: In uniform and with a gun. That was the German frontier. But Italian frontier was quite different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was Switzerland. And I stayed there to be able to catch my boat in Naples, which came from Southampton, where I was not allowed to land, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's unbelievable. Anyway, I went on the boat. I had a nice cabin to myself because my girlfriend looked after that I get a good ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bronia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronia. She was absolutely gorgeous. And the boat went normal way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only immigrants on it, like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. I mean, I never worried much about the conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you make friends on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the whole-- the whole boat, we were all very friendly with each other, all Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Full with Jewish people. And on one Sunday, we come to Singapore. Now comes a story which is hard to believe, but it's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Singapore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't even know what you were going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a shock?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And it was very hot. That-- that I remember. Anyway, we anchored in Singapore. It was a Sunday. And all of a sudden, I am called to-- to come to the office of the boat. There was a big, round table and consisting of men only, Jewish men. They took people from the boat. They took engineers, doctors in Singapore. What people there needed, European people there needed, for their city of Singapore. And then there came to me, young girl, 20 years of age, alone. They wanted to-- I was called to come and see.

speaker: interviewer
text: There I see a big table, about six or eight men sitting there. And one got up and said, what is your name? And I told him my name. He said, mhm. And how do you like it here? I said to him, I just came. I don't know. [LAUGHS] It's all right. Where are you going? I said, we are going to Shanghai. He said, mm. He said, you know what? I'd like to take you home to my home for afternoon tea. So I said, look, I have all my friends here on board. I don't know you. I don't know who you are, I said in very broken English.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, that's all right. He said, you can invite the whole boat if you like. I don't care. I have enough room. And I'll have you all for afternoon tea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to the end of the boat, and he made this. [CLAPPING] And the cars came, the most beautiful cars. Name a few. Well, I suppose Rolls-Royces. Yeah. Well, one was Rolls-Royce. Jaguars and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another one was-- and I took about 20 people or 25 people with me to go to his place for afternoon tea. [LAUGHS] And we got there. And everybody looked at me from the boat, and they said, listen, aren't we lucky to get such a nice invitation? Anyway, we came to his house. And the whole tea was already laid out, cups, and saucers and biscuits and whatever you want, and water and everything. It was a hot afternoon, but it was all covered. Not with air conditioning, naturally. The heat was naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden appears the officer-- the officer who does give permission to stay or not to stay. What do you call him? Immigration officer maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immigration officer. He had to give his approval. Because Mr. Penhaus-- that was his name-- wanted me to stay with his family at least for a fortnight, and I can take the next boat to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they came down frequently. And I wasn't very willing until one of them kicked me and said, are you crazy? Of course you do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you know what they did? They had a lovely little house near the beach, belonging also to his estate, which he had. And-- [LAUGHS] and two eunuchs were standing in front of my door at night. Two eunuchs to guard you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know why he chose you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had blue eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had never seen a Jewish girl with blue eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the truth, the utter truth, what I am telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the family good to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Flora, his sister, was not so pleased with me. Because Flora wasn't married. And they really wanted so much to get her, because he wanted to marry me, so-- so I heard later on. He really wanted to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a single man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aha. And he wanted to marry--

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a younger brother too. He wanted to marry me, too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't want to marry him.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he let you go after two weeks, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't let me go at all. And papers were called. Papers came to see me and the Penhaus family. I mean, it was-- I was quite famous without knowing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A celebrity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, completely-- unbelievable. Well, the boat left at night. And they begged me and begged me. And one of the boys said-- from the boat said, look, this is a fantastic offer. Take it. You can come in a fortnight to-- to Shanghai. Shanghai doesn't run away. And the next day and the day after, the press was coming and going and photographing me. And papers were full. And you have no idea. Telephone was ringing. And it was unbelievable-- unbelievable. And I was staying in my separate little house with the two eunuchs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first time, the second time, I went to play tennis. And-- mo, I was invited-- another young man I knew, who was a far-related cousin of mine saw my picture in the paper in Singapore and rang me at-- Penhaus is the name of the people I stayed with-- and rang me there and said, what are you doing there?

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know he was there. He didn't know I come-- I emigrated to Shanghai or whatever. And he said, look, tonight I have a dinner party. And I need a woman for one single gentleman. And well, Abe-- that is the gentleman that took me off the boat; his name was Abe-- was not very keen to let me go to another party. And he said, all right, I can go. I'll take you there, and I pick you up at half past 10. So-- [LAUGHS] he took me there to that party. And this gentleman opposite me was my late husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So you met him then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him then for the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a Jewish guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his profession, or was he too young?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an engineer and architect.

speaker: interviewer
text: An engineer and architect.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was working for the Sultan of Johor. You've heard of Johor?

speaker: interviewer
text: I certainly have.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Sultan of Johor, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the Sultan of Johor was his client at the time. And had-- he built-- they're still standing-- little houses for all his wives and what have you, staff and-- in Johor. And my late husband--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met there, and that was-- he-- straightaway you liked each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes. He was very, very nice. First of all, he was not half dark, you know. He was-- and not so terribly religious, very religious business. And I was really non-- I didn't-- I didn't have any Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you had not much affiliation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much affiliation with the Jewish religion at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so did you start to go out with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Mr. Penhaus [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Pennhaus didn't like me--

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't like you doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not very keen.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So still he let you go out?

speaker: interviewee
text: He let me go out, yes. He had to come to the house, and he had to bring me back to the house, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. And was he allowed in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for how long did you have a courtship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember. Not very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when he proposed to me, I first of all didn't understand him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: My English wasn't good enough. [LAUGHS] And-- but he went to Mr. Penhaus, to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: To ask for your hand in marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did Mr. Penhaus say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very reluctantly, he said to him-- he told me later on. I wasn't present, of course. He told me later on that he was very, very heartbroken, because he had a sister. Penhaus had a sister. She was the ugliest duckling under the sun. You have never seen-- and she was not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was Flora?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Flora. And Flora didn't like me. And Flora-- but they were frantically looking for a husband for Flora and couldn't succeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we have to stop for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with Leonie Hilton. It's the 7th of February, 1996. And we're in Sydney, Australia
